coredata.dc:title: Efficiency enhancement of hybridized solar cells through co-sensitization and fast charge extraction by up-converted polyethylene glycol modified carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Wanlu
Duan, Jialong
Duan, Yanyan
Zhao, Yuanyuan
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: Photovoltaics are promising solutions to energy crisis and environmental pollution problems. The dye-sensitized solar cells with mesoscopic structures have attracted growing interests because of zero emissions, easy fabrication, scalable materials and techniques, etc. However, the state-of-the-art dye-sensitized solar cells have narrow spectral absorption for photoelectric conversion and high electron-hole recombination rate under sunlight illumination. Therefore, it is a persistent object to make wide-spectral absorption and fast charge extraction solar cells for energy harvest in both solar and dark-light conditions. To address this issue, we present here experimental realization of a category of solar cells converting visible and near-infrared light into electricity by co-sensitizing photoanode with N719 dye and polyethylene glycol (PEG) modified carbon quantum dots (PEG-m-CQDs), arising from up-conversion and hole-transporting behaviors of PEG-m-CQDs as well as photofluorescence of green-emitting long persistence phosphors. The optimized solar cell yields maximized photoelectric conversion efficiencies of 9.89% and 25.81% under simulated sunlight (air mass 1.5, 100 mW cm−2) illumination and dark conditions, respectively. This work is far from optimization, but the physical proof-of-concept hybridized solar cell may markedly increase electricity generation time and total power output of photovoltaic platforms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitized solar cell
Up-conversion
Light absorption
Solar energy utilization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CoSe CE and co-sensitized photoanodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Formation mechanism of PEG-m-CQDs. (b) The photographs of the CQDs (upper) and PEG-m-CQDs (lower) taken under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (right). (c) TEM images of the PEG-m-CQDs and the size distribution (inset) and (d) HRTEM images of the PEG-m-CQDs. The inset shows the FFT image corresponding to the selected area.
The (a) XPS and (b) FTIR spectra of PEG-m-CQDs.

(a) UV-vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of PEG-m-CQDs synthesized at different hydrothermal times. The PL spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 800 nm. The (c) down- and (d) up-converted PL emission spectra of the optimized PEG-m-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths, the inset show the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra.

(a) Schematic diagram of the co-sensitized hybridized solar cells, and (b) energy level distribution and charge transfer processes at photoanode/electrolyte interface. Characteristic J-V curves for various photovoltaics (c) under simulated solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2, AM1.5) and (d) in dark conditions. (e) Nyquist and (f) Bode EIS phase plots of the solar cells.

(a) The optical transmission curves of FTO substrate and transparent CoSe electrode. (b) The optical transmission curves of various photoanodes and their solar cells. (c) The PL emission spectra of photofluorescent LPP|CoSe CE excited under various wavelengths. (d) The photoluminescence image of photofluorescent LPP|CoSe CE taken at different aftreglow times after irradiation by a simulated sunlight (air mass 1.5, 100 mW cm−2) for 3 min.
The dark (a) P max, (b) J sc, (c) V oc and (d) FF as a function of afterglow time.
The photo and dark photovoltaic parameters for corresponding solar cells. η: power conversion efficiency; J sc: short-circuit current density; V oc: open-circuit voltage; FF: fill factor.
The charge-transfer resistance at photoanode|electrolyte, peak frequency (f p) and electron lifetime (τ = 1/2πf p).

head.ce:title: Efficiency enhancement of hybridized solar cells through co-sensitization and fast charge extraction by up-converted polyethylene glycol modified carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photovoltaics are promising solutions to energy crisis and environmental pollution problems. The dye-sensitized solar cells with mesoscopic structures have attracted growing interests because of zero emissions, easy fabrication, scalable materials and techniques, etc. However, the state-of-the-art dye-sensitized solar cells have narrow spectral absorption for photoelectric conversion and high electron-hole recombination rate under sunlight illumination. Therefore, it is a persistent object to make wide-spectral absorption and fast charge extraction solar cells for energy harvest in both solar and dark-light conditions. To address this issue, we present here experimental realization of a category of solar cells converting visible and near-infrared light into electricity by co-sensitizing photoanode with N719 dye and polyethylene glycol (PEG) modified carbon quantum dots (PEG-m-CQDs), arising from up-conversion and hole-transporting behaviors of PEG-m-CQDs as well as photofluorescence of green-emitting long persistence phosphors. The optimized solar cell yields maximized photoelectric conversion efficiencies of 9.89% and 25.81% under simulated sunlight (air mass 1.5, 100 mW cm−2) illumination and dark conditions, respectively. This work is far from optimization, but the physical proof-of-concept hybridized solar cell may markedly increase electricity generation time and total power output of photovoltaic platforms.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar cells are photoelectric devices for converting solar energy into electricity with zero emissions [1–3]. Among several potential solar cells, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have attracted tremendous scientific interests because of simplicity in their fabrication procedures and advances in technical aspects [4]. Until now, the narrow light absorption spectral of commercial N719 dye molecules, sluggish hole transportation from N719 to redox electrolyte, and sunlight-dependent conversion are regarded as the main barriers for the development of these photovoltaic devices. Various solar cell architectures have been applied to harvest extraordinary solar-energy from nature, and therefore increasing the power output. Li et al. have designed an integrated photovoltaic device assisted with a fluorescent concentrator for enhancing the response to sunlight [5]. Peng et al. have realized omnidirectional absorption via assembling a DSSC in a light weight waveguide fiber [6]. In addition to these light-harvesting designs, some inorganic quantum dots (QDs) that can absorb near-infrared light are always used as light absorbers, such as PbS [7,8] and GaSb [9]. However, the toxicity of these QDs has limited their applications in photovoltaics. Arising from lower toxicity [10], size- and synthesis-dependent optical absorption [11], and multiple exciton generation effects [12], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are ideal alternative light absorbers in solar cells [13–19]. In fact, quantum dot-sensitized solar cells with CQD-only sensitizers yield lower power conversion efficiencies because of the weak affinity between CQDs and mesoscopic TiO2 [20,21]. Moreover, the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) of N719 dye is −5.45 eV in comparison to potential (−4.9 eV) of redox I−/I3 − electrolyte, giving a large energy difference of 0.55 eV and therefore limited charges (electrons, holes) extraction. One of the solutions to this impasse is to improve interfacial charge extractions in competing with carrier trapping and even thermalization/cooling.

Another bottleneck problem for current solar cells is that they can only output electricity under sunlight illumination, while the power generation is low or zero in foggy, rainy and night etc dark-light conditions. Therefore, a challenging but promising topic is to realize persistent electricity-generation in all weathers [22]. Recently, we have taken the first step to create bifunctional all-weather DSSCs tailored by graphene [23], graphene based coating films [24] or platinum alloys [25] to harvest solar and rain energies. The final solar cells yield maximized photoelectric conversion efficiency of 10.38% [25] under solar irradiation as well as current of 5.97 μA and voltage of 227.8 μV under simulated rain droplets [24]. A remaining problem is that the above-mentioned solar cells are still unavailable at rain-free nights and other dark-light environments.

By addressing above-mentioned issues, it is desirable to study interfacial engineering of DSSCs by setting environment-friendly QDs at anode/electrolyte interface, acting as light converter and superfast bridge to facilitate hole injection from anode to redox electrolyte. In the current work, polyethylene glycol (PEG) molecules are used for CQDs passivation, realizing down- and up-conversion under excitation of ultraviolet (UV) and near infrared (NIR) light, respectively. The experimental results have shown that the power conversion efficiency of photovoltaic device is markedly enhanced using co-sensitizers from PEG-m-CQDs and N719 dye in comparison with N719-only based DSSC. The mechanism behind this enhancement is that the PEG-m-CQDs widens light absorption ranging from visible to NIR regions as well as fast hole extraction for efficient electron-hole separation. Moreover, transparent metal selenide counter electrode (CE) is modified by green-emitting long persistence phosphors (LPPs) to harvest scattering light from surrounding atmosphere and emit green photofluorescence for several hours at dark-light conditions. In this fashion, this fluorescent counter electrode may be considered as a “light absorber” in bright conditions and a “photosource” in dark-light environments.

CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using glucose as a raw material. In details, 2 g of glucose was heated at 195 ± 5 °C using a heating mantle for 6 min until the liquid was changed from colorless to orange. Subsequently, the resultant solution was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water to obtain CQD aqueous solution.

0.2 g of PEG (M w = 4000) was added into 30 mL of CQD aqueous solution, and vigorously agitated until complete dissolution.Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 2, 4, 6, 8 or 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was subjected to filtration, centrifugation (8000 rpm) and dialysis in a dialysis bag (3500 D) for 3 days and the final PEG-m-CQDs aqueous solution was thus obtained. Subsequently, the PEG-m-CQDs aqueous solution was freeze-dried under vacuum to obtain solid powers which were dispersed in ethyl alcohol to form 1 mg mL−1 solution for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CoSe CE and co-sensitized photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The CoSe alloy CE and TiO2 colloid were prepared according to our previous reports [26]. The rear side of CoSe alloy CE was coated by ultrafine green-emitting LPP phosphors, which were mixed in the glue and then solidified in air for 12 h to obtain the LPP|CoSe CE. Colloidal TiO2 films was fabricated by coating colloid onto freshly cleaned FTO glass substrates with 0.25 cm2 by a doctor-blade method and then calcined at 450 °C for 30 min in air. The resultant mesoscopic TiO2 anodes were sensitized by a 0.50 mM N719 ethanol solution for 24 h to obtain dye-sensitized N719 photoanodes. Then the N719-sensitized photoanode was spin-coated by PEG-m-CQDs ethyl alcohol solution for 30 s at a speed of 2000 rpm to obtain N719/PEG-m-CQDs co-sensitized photoanodes.

Each solar cell device was built by sandwiching a I−/I3 − redox electrolyte between a CE and a photoanode. A liquid electrolyte consists of 0.1 M tetraethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M tetramethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium iodide, 0.1 M NaI, 0.1 M KI, 0.1 M LiI, 0.05 M I2 and 0.05 M 4-tert-butyl-pyridine in acetonitrile. The photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves of various solar cells with an active area of 0.25 cm2 were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical work station under irradiation of simulated solar light from an intensity controlled to 100 mW cm−2 xenon arc lamp. Before measurement for dark J-V curves, the solar cells were illuminated by a simulated solar light for 1 min. Immediately, the devices were covered in a completely dark condition and the J-V curves were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation in air. To calculate the dark efficiency, the fluorescent light intensity of LPP|CoSe CE was recorded by a standard silicon solar cell. Therefore, the dark cell efficiency was obtained according to η = P max/P in, P in was the fluorescent light intensity of LPP|CoSe CE.

Fig. 1 a shows the synthesis process of PEG passivated CQDs using glucose as a raw material through bottom-up routes. Upon dehydration process through pyrolyzing reaction, glucose molecules are converted into CQDs through nucleation and epitaxial growth processes, which can be explained in our previous work [20]. Subsequently, the CQDs liquid suffer a hydrothermal treatment at 170 °C in the presence of PEG for 6 h. The final PEG-m-CQDs aqueous solution has a brighter blue luminescence than CQDs under excitation by a 365 nm UV lamp, as shown in Fig. 1b. The phenomenon of enhanced fluorescence after CQDs surface-modified has also been reported in other literature [27], arising from higher photo-to-electron conversion capability. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of PEG-m-CQDs in Fig. 1c reveals that the PEG-m-CQDs are homogeneously dispersed in aqueous solution with an average diameter of 2–3 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1d) shows a well-defined crystalline structure with the lattice fringe distance of 0.232 nm, which is attributable to (100) diffraction plane of graphitic structure. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (insert) indicates a hexagonal structure for as-prepared CQDs.

A possible formation mechanism of PEG-m-CQDs is the interaction between carboxyl groups of CQDs and hydroxyl groups of PEG molecules under hydrothermal conditions. Therefore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) characterizations are performed to explore the chemical bonds and elemental composition of PEG-m-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2 a. Five peaks at 284.4, 284.9, 286.0, 286.5 and 287.5 eV are extracted by splitting C1s spectrum, corresponding to C=C, C-C, C-O, C=O and O-C=O groups [28,29], respectively. The presence of above-mentioned groups is cross-checked with FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2b), in which the absorption peaks centered at 1468, 1608 and 1105 cm−1 are ascribed to the C=C stretching, C=O stretching and C-O-C stretching, respectively. Meanwhile, the -OH stretching, C-H stretching and C-H out-of-plane bending modes are determined in PEG-m-CQDs at 3357, 2891, and 845 cm−1. Clearly, the basic framework of PEG-m-CQDs comprises of C=C bonds, revealing that the PEG-m-CQDs have a cyclic aromatic hydrocarbon-like structure for π electron transition. Some oxygenic groups such as –OH, -COOH, -C=O, C-O-C and -COOH groups are decorated in in-plane polyaromatic structure or outward surface of PEG-m-CQDs.

In order to better understand the electronic structures, PEG-m-CQDs are subjected to spectral characterizations. As shown in Fig. 3 a, two characteristic absorption peak centered at ca. 224 nm and 282 nm are observed in UV-vis absorption spectra, corresponding to π → π∗ of C=C segment [30,31] and n → π∗ transition of C=O bond [31,32], respectively. Intriguingly, the peak intensities of PEG-m-CQDs both increase with prolonged reaction time, whereas the characteristic peak centers are nearly unchanged, which is mainly attributed to the increased PEG-m-CQDs content in aqueous solution with increasing of reaction time [31]. Fig. 3b shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of PEG-m-CQDs excited by 800 nm-NIR light and the up-converted emission wavelengths are located at about 475 nm. There is a peak PL intensity for the PEG-m-CQDs synthesized at 6 h, which is over 7 times higher than CQDs without surface passivation. The PL spectra of PEG-m-CQDs at a reaction time of 6 h under excitation wavelengths of 350–490 nm and 725–950 nm are shown in Fig. 3c and d. All the PL spectra have a maximal PL intensity peak located at about 445 nm, and then shift to longer wavelength along with decreased intensities as the elevation of excitation wavelength. In details, the PL emission peaks shift from 442 to 539 nm upon excitation from 350 to 490 nm, as shown in Fig. 3c. The single PL peak, arising from emissive traps, electronic conjugated structure, and free zigzag sites, is due to the existence of a single electron transition pathway with an electronic structure of PEG-m-CQDs [31]. Similarly, the PL emission peaks are shifted from 445 to 533 nm by tuning excitation wavelength from 725 to 950 nm, as shown in Fig. 3d. The phenomenon of emission wavelength and intensity dependent on excitation wavelength has also observed in other carbon-based quantum dots [33–35]. The up-converted ability of the PEG-m-CQDs should be attributed to an anti-Stokes transition [34] or multiphoton active process [35–37], in which the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons leads to the emission of light at a short wavelength than that of the excitation. Due to the strong luminescence and photo-excitation by NIR competences of PEG-m-CQDs, they are expected to be applied in solar cells as light absorbers, widening spectral absorption and therefore enhancing photovoltaic performances.

The synthesized PEG-m-CQDs are used to modify N719-TiO2/electrolyte interface, and the so-called hybridized solar cell devices are made according to the layout in Fig. 4 a, and corresponding relative energy level diagram of different layers is displayed in Fig. 4b. By performing cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement [38] (Fig. S1), the energy levels of HOMO and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of PEG-m-CQDs synthesized at a reaction time of 6 h are determined to be −5.01 and −3.88 eV below vacuum lever, respectively. The matching HOMO edge (−5.01 eV) of PEG-m-CQDs to HOMO value (−5.45 eV) of dye and redox potential of I−/I3 − couples (−4.9 eV) demonstrates a bridge for extracting hole from N719 to electrolyte, leaving efficient electron-hole separation and increased voltage output. In addition, the narrow energy bandgap (1.13 eV) of PEG-m-CQDs makes it possible to absorb NIR light below 1097 nm and to convert into visible light for re-absorption by N719 dye. In this fashion, the total current output in final solar cells may be elevated. However, the lower LUMO level (−3.88 eV) in comparison to −3.85 eV for N719 dye suggests that the photogenerated electrons from PEG-m-CQDs are not collected by N719-TiO2 photoanode. Therefore, PEG-m-CQDs play two roles in final device: converting NIR into visible light and extracting holes from N719 dye to redox electrolyte.

Fig. 4c shows the current (J)-voltage (V) characteristics of various solar cells under simulated air mass 1.5 global (AM1.5) solar illumination and photovoltaics parameters are summarized in Table 1 . The standard N719-TiO2/electrolyte/CE solar cell achieves a photoelectric conversion efficiency of 7.80% (V oc = 0.703 V, J sc = 15.77 mA cm−2, FF = 70.4%), and it increases to 8.19% in the device tailored with N719 and PEG-m-CQDs. An increased J sc from 15.77 to 17.01 mA cm−2 and V oc from 0.703 to 0.708 V cross-checked the wide-spectral absorption and fast charge extractions. From monochromatic incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra for various solar cells, one can find that the photoelectric conversion efficiency of co-DSSC is higher than that for pristine DSSC (Fig. S2). Additionally, the photocurrent density versus time plots demonstrate that the co-DSSC photocurrent stability upon prolonged exposure to light irradiation (100 mW cm−2) is better than pristine DSSC (Fig. S3). The potential mechanisms behind these enhancements may be explained by three reasons: (i) The PEG-m-CQDs are light up-converting scaffolds from NIR to visible light, which is subsequently re-absorbed by N719 dye for more electron excitation (Fig. S4); (ii) The PEG-m-CQDs can extract photogenerated holes from HOMO band of N719 to redox electrolyte, markedly increasing electron-hole separation; (iii) quasi-2D structure of PEG-m-CQDs barriers backward recombination reactions between oxidized species (I3 −) in electrolyte and photogenerated electrons at photoanode/electrolyte interface, which can be confirmed by reduced dark current (Fig. 4d) and charge-transfer resistance as well as increased electron lifetime. EIS Nyquist and Bode phase plots in Fig. 4e and f are recorded to compare the electrochemical processes of standard and co-sensitized DSSC devices. The values of charge-transfer resistance (R ct2) and lifetime (τ) of electrons at photoanode/electrolyte interface are summarized in Table 2 . Obviously, the co-DSSC has lower R ct2 at photoanode/electrolyte (4.8 Ω cm2) comparing with the pristine DSSC (7.69 Ω cm2), suggesting that the charge recombination reactions in co-DSSC device have been restricted in comparison with those in DSSC. V oc is always determined by the energy difference between Fermi energy level of TiO2 and redox potential of I−/I3 − couples, however the real V oc output is lower than theoretical value because of backward recombination reactions. According to the above-mentioned discussion, the co-DSSC device can yield higher V oc than DSSC, which is consistent with J-V performances. Meanwhile, the lifetime of photo-electron can be calculated according to τ = 1/2πf p, where f p represents the peak frequency as shown in Fig. 4f. A lager τ suggests that the electrons suffer limited backward recombination reactions with I3 − species, leading to increased electrons density at conduction band of TiO2 for J sc output. The value of τ at co-DSSC is 7.4 ms, which is much larger than 2.8 ms for DSSC, demonstrating the suppressive recombination reactions in the solar cells.

From the J-V curves for standard or co-sensitized DSSC devices, as shown in Fig. 4b, the current and voltage outputs are zero because of visible light-free dark conditions. This represents a challenging problem that state-of-the-art solar cells do not have the capability of persistently generating power in dark-light atmosphere such as nights. With an aim of prolonging generating time and therefore total power outputs, we present here the building of photofluorescent CoSe CE to harvest scattering light energy for monochromatic green emission. The final solar cell architecture is shown in Fig. 4a, including a TiO2|dye|PEG-m-CQDs photoanode, a redox electrolyte having I−/I3 − redox couples, and an FTO glass supported CoSe/LPP (LPP|CoSe) photofluorescent CE. This so-called green-emitting CE is made on a ∼60%-transparency CoSe electrode (Fig. 5 a), and the power conversion efficiency in fluorescent LPP|CoSe CE tailored co-sensitized DSSC is increased to 9.89% under AM1.5 irradiation. Surprisingly, a J sc of 84 μA cm−2, a V oc of 0.304 V and an FF of 60.6% are determined from the dark J-V characteristics, yielding a dark efficiency of 25.81% according to η = (P max/P input) × 100%, where P input is fluorescence intensity of LPP|CoSe CE and characterized by a standard Si-solar cell based irradiatometer. The mechanism behind substantial increase in efficiency is attributed to irradiation of simulated sunlight from front side and fluorescent light of LPP|CE from rear side. When suffering sunlight illumination, the LPP|CoSe CE can store optical energy from front solar cell as well as from surrounding environment, and subsequently release green fluorescence by trapping-storage-releasing mechanisms. The unabsorbed visible and infrared light beyond about 550 nm by N719 dye and PEG-m-CQDs can penetrate the whole solar cell device (ca. 41.4%) (see Fig. 5b, orange line), and subsequently be stored in LPP|CoSe CE (see Fig. 5b, green line). Finally, the LPP|CoSe CE can emit green fluorescence ranging from 450 to 600 nm, which can be re-absorbed by N719 and PEG-m-CQDs photosensitizers (Fig. S5) across transparent CoSe electrode and electrolyte. In this way, a new strategy to enhance efficiency of solar cells by converting full solar spectrum into visible light can be realized. After ceasing simulated sunlight illumination, the afterglow time can last for several hours, as shown in Fig. 5d. By plotting persistent luminescence intensity versus decay time, as shown in Fig. S6, one can find that the maximal emission intensity is 129.3 μW cm−2 for photofluorescent LPP|CoSe CE, subsequently, the afterglow intensity gradually decrease with time to an equilibrium state. Highlighted by this feature, the so-called hybridized solar cells have an ability of persistently generate power in the dark.

The fluorescence intensity from LPP|CoSe CE decreases when exposing in dark-light conditions, therefore long-term duration of generating electricity is a crucial factor to promote solar cells' practical applications. As shown in Fig. 5d, we have captured the photoluminescence images of LPP|CoSe CE at different decay times when ceasing incident light. No matter decay for seconds or even 2 h, the fluorescent CE is still brilliant. By recording dark J-V curves (Fig. S7) and plotting P max, J sc, V oc, or FF as a function of decay time (Fig. 6 ), all photovoltaic parameters have abrupt reductions in the first 7 min and tend to nearly constants in nest 113 min. The initial P max, J sc, V oc, and FF for co-DSSC with LPP|CoSe CE are reduced from 15.486 μW cm−2, 84 μA cm−2, 0.304 V and 60.6% to 1.241 μW cm−2, 20.5 μA cm−2, 0.194 V and 31.2% after a decay time of 2 h, respectively. Nevertheless, the parameters of the solar cells that are generated at dark condition can be improved by optimizing the luminescent properties of LPP phosphors [39].

In summary, we have successfully made a hybridized solar cell prototype co-sensitized by N719 dye and PEG-m-CQDs. Using the up-conversion fast charge extraction abilities of PEG-m-CQDs as well as photofluorescent behaviors of LPP|CoSe CE, the so-called hybridized solar cell yields a photo efficiency of 9.89% and a dark efficiency of 25.81%. This finding highlights the beneficial role of PEG-m-CQDs in widening absorption spectral and facilitating electron-hole separation along with physical proof-of-concept for solar cells. Future work on developing more robust CQDs and fundamental principles in both photovoltaic building and energy harvest from nature may open up new pathways of new generation solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (61774139, 21503202 and 61604143). Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2015EM024) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (201762018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2017.09.055.
